questions.question_text: How much does each Alabama license applicant pay into the Recovery Fund?
questions.explanation: Each applicant for an original real estate license in Alabama pays a $30 fee into the Recovery Fund. This is a one-time fee at initial licensure. If the fund balance falls below $500,000, the commission may order each broker and salesperson to pay a $30 fee for each license held.

questions.question_text: The commission may order every Alabama broker and salesperson to pay a $30 fee per license when the Recovery Fund balance falls below:
questions.explanation: When the Recovery Fund balance falls below $500,000, the commission may order each broker and salesperson to pay a $30 fee for each license held. There is no general minimum-balance requirement for the fund. The $600,000 figure is only the floor protecting the fund under a one-time transfer provision benefiting the University of Alabama.

questions.question_text: The Alabama Recovery Fund compensates parties who suffer losses due to the acts of:
distractors: Any party in a real estate transaction
questions.explanation: The Recovery Fund covers losses caused by licensed brokers or salespersons. Claims cannot be filed against unlicensed or inactive licensees. The fund covers only actual/compensatory damages, not interest or court costs.

questions.question_text: Which type of damages does the Alabama Recovery Fund cover?
questions.explanation: The Recovery Fund covers only actual/compensatory damages. It does NOT cover interest, court costs, attorney fees, or punitive damages. This limitation keeps the fund focused on making injured parties financially whole.

questions.question_text: A buyer loses money on a transaction conducted by an unlicensed individual. Can they file a Recovery Fund claim?
questions.correct_answer: No, claims require a licensed party
distractors: Yes, the fund covers all transactions
Yes, but only for actual damages
questions.explanation: Recovery Fund claims can only be filed against licensed brokers or salespersons. If the person who caused the loss was unlicensed or had an inactive license, the claim will be denied. The claimant would need to pursue other legal remedies.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum Recovery Fund payment per transaction in Alabama?
questions.explanation: The maximum payment from the Alabama Recovery Fund is $25,000 per transaction, regardless of how many claimants are involved. The aggregate limit per licensee is $50,000 for all claims combined.

questions.question_text: Three separate buyers each lost money due to the same licensee's misconduct across different transactions. What is the total maximum they can collectively recover from the fund?
questions.explanation: The aggregate maximum per licensee is $50,000, regardless of the number of transactions or claimants. Each individual transaction is capped at $25,000. So three separate claims totaling more than $50,000 would be limited to the $50,000 aggregate cap.

questions.question_text: A licensee's spouse files a Recovery Fund claim against the licensee. What is the likely outcome?
distractors: The claim will be approved if valid
The claim requires AREC hearing first
questions.explanation: Claims by a child, spouse, or parent against their own family member are denied under Alabama Recovery Fund rules. This prevents family members from using the fund for intra-family disputes.

questions.question_text: A Florida resident suffers a loss due to an Alabama licensee's actions on a property located in Florida. Can they file a Recovery Fund claim?
questions.correct_answer: No, damages must occur within Alabama
distractors: Yes, if the licensee is Alabama-licensed
Yes, for any transaction involving an AL licensee
questions.explanation: The Alabama Recovery Fund only covers damages sustained within Alabama. Even if the licensee holds an Alabama license, the loss must have occurred in connection with Alabama real estate to be eligible for a claim.

questions.question_text: What is the per-transaction limit of the Alabama Recovery Fund regardless of the number of claimants?
questions.explanation: The $25,000 per-transaction limit is an aggregate cap — it applies regardless of how many people file claims related to that single transaction. If five people are harmed in one transaction, they share the $25,000 maximum.

questions.question_text: When must an aggrieved person notify the Alabama Real Estate Commission that they have filed suit against a licensee?
questions.correct_answer: At the commencement of the action, by certified mail
distractors: Within 10 days after obtaining a final judgment
Only after all appeals have concluded
questions.explanation: There is no month-based filing deadline for Recovery Fund claims. Instead, when an aggrieved person commences an action against a licensee, they must notify the commission in writing by certified mail. The verified claim for payment is then filed after final judgment and the conclusion of all appeals, on 10 days' written notice to the commission.

questions.question_text: Before filing a Recovery Fund claim, the claimant must first:
questions.correct_answer: Obtain a final court judgment against the licensee
distractors: Attempt to collect from the licensee
questions.explanation: Alabama requires the claimant to obtain a final court judgment against the licensee before applying for payment from the Recovery Fund. There is no requirement to first attempt to collect from the licensee. The claimant must also show they are not a family member of the licensee, that any bonding companies were joined in the action, and that amounts already recovered have been applied to the loss.

questions.question_text: A payment is made from the Recovery Fund due to a licensee's misconduct. What happens to the licensee's license?
questions.correct_answer: It may be terminated by the commission
distractors: It remains active with a reprimand
questions.explanation: When the Recovery Fund pays a claim based on a licensee's conduct, all of the licensee's licenses may be terminated by the commission — termination is discretionary, not automatic. Before a new license is issued, the licensee must repay the fund in full plus 12 percent annual interest, and a bankruptcy discharge does not relieve this obligation.

questions.question_text: A licensee's licenses were terminated after a Recovery Fund payment. Before the licensee can receive a new license, they must:
questions.correct_answer: Repay the fund in full plus 12% annual interest
distractors: Repay only the original amount paid from the fund
Have the debt discharged in bankruptcy
questions.explanation: When the fund pays on a licensee's account, the licensee's licenses may be terminated, and the commission may refuse to issue a new license until the fund has been repaid in full plus 12 percent annual interest. Repaying only the original amount is not sufficient, and a discharge in bankruptcy does not relieve the obligation to the fund.

questions.question_text: After the Recovery Fund pays a claim on a judgment against a licensee, what right does the commission acquire?
questions.correct_answer: It is subrogated to the judgment creditor's rights
distractors: A lien on all real property owned by the licensee
The right to recover the payment from the claimant
No additional rights — the fund payment is final
questions.explanation: When the Recovery Fund pays a claim, the commission is subrogated to the rights of the judgment creditor. This means the commission steps into the claimant's shoes and may pursue the licensee to recover the amount paid from the fund.
